file_name: Zounds Hearing, Inc. - MANUFACTURING DESIGN MARKETING AGREEMENT.txt

perturbation.original_text: In the event Manufacturer in good faith believes that Manufacturer's Products are nonconforming under the specifications agreed to among the Parties (the "Standards"), Manufacturer shall give written notice to Subcontractor specifying in detail the nonconformity within thirty (30) days of Manufacturer's receipt of such Manufacturer's Products. Upon Manufacturer's request, Subcontractor will facilitate such Manufacturer's Products being repaired or replaced, Manufacturer must return the Manufacturer's Products to Subcontractor, transportation charges prepaid by Manufacturer, within fifteen (15) days of the end of such thirty (30) date notice period.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event Manufacturer in good faith believes that Manufacturer's Products are nonconforming under the specifications agreed to among the Parties (the "Standards"), Manufacturer shall give written notice to Subcontractor specifying in detail the nonconformity within ten (10) days of Manufacturer's receipt of such Manufacturer's Products. Upon Manufacturer's request, Subcontractor will facilitate such Manufacturer's Products being repaired or replaced, Manufacturer must return the Manufacturer's Products to Subcontractor, transportation charges prepaid by Manufacturer, within five (5) days of the end of such ten (10) date notice period.
perturbation.explanation: This change shortens the notification and return periods for nonconforming products, potentially violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under Arizona law, which requires parties to act reasonably and honestly in performing their contractual obligations. The shortened timeframes may not allow the manufacturer sufficient time to adequately assess and report nonconformities.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: Under Arizona law, every contract contains an implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. This means both parties must act reasonably and honestly. Shortening the time to notify and return defective goods to 10 days could be seen as unreasonable and not providing enough time for the Manufacturer to assess the products it received, violating this covenant.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 12-341.01 - Recovery of attorney fees 12-341.01 . Recovery of attorney fees A. In any contested action arising out of a contract, express or implied, the court may award the successful party reasonable attorney fees.Â If a written settlement offer is rejected and the judgment finally obtained is equal to or more favorable to the offeror than an offer made in writing to settle any contested action arising out of a contract, the offeror is deemed to be the successful party from the date of the offer and the court may award the successful party reasonable attorney fees. This section shall not be construed as altering, prohibiting or restricting present or future contracts or statutes that may provide for attorney fees. B. The award of reasonable attorney fees pursuant to this section should be made to mitigate the burden of the expense of litigation to establish a just claim or a just defense. It need not equal or relate to the attorney fees actually paid or contracted, but the award may not exceed the amount paid or agreed to be paid. C. The court and not a jury shall award reasonable attorney fees under this section.

perturbation.justification: The changed text shortens the notification and return periods for nonconforming products from 30 to 10 days and 15 to 5 days respectively. The perturbation explanation and law explanation both suggest that this may violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under Arizona law, by not providing enough time for the Manufacturer to adequately assess the products it received. While the provided law snippets concern attorney fees and don't directly address good faith, the explanations provided focus on the timeframe being 'unreasonable', which aligns with the definition of 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction', specifically, setting 'a deadline or requirement that violates federal or state law' (in this case, implied covenant) which would make the 'contractual terms unenforceable or illegal'.

perturbation.original_text: During the term hereof and for a period of five (5) years thereafter, each Party agrees not to, either directly or indirectly, for itself or on behalf of any other person, firm, partnership, corporation or other entity hire, solicit, contract for, attempt to solicit, or cause to be solicited, the employment or services of any current or previous employee of the other Party (unless a period of sixty months has elapsed from the last date that such employee was employed by such party) without the prior written consent of such other Party.
perturbation.changed_text: During the term hereof and for a period of ten (10) years thereafter, each Party agrees not to, either directly or indirectly, for itself or on behalf of any other person, firm, partnership, corporation or other entity hire, solicit, contract for, attempt to solicit, or cause to be solicited, the employment or services of any current or previous employee of the other Party (unless a period of one hundred twenty months has elapsed from the last date that such employee was employed by such party) without the prior written consent of such other Party.
perturbation.explanation: This modification extends the non-solicitation period to 10 years. While Arizona law permits non-solicitation agreements, a 10-year restriction may be deemed unreasonable and an unlawful restraint of trade, especially if it unduly restricts an employee's ability to find employment. Arizona courts consider the scope, duration, and geographic area of such restrictions.
perturbation.law_explanation: A.R.S. § 44-1405 addresses restraints of trade. While it doesn't explicitly prohibit non-solicitation agreements, Arizona courts scrutinize them for reasonableness. A ten-year non-solicitation clause is facially unreasonable, potentially violating Arizona's policy against restraints of trade because it is more than what is necessary to protect the legitimate business interests.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 44-1405 - Judicial jurisdiction 44-1405 . Judicial jurisdiction An action for violation of this article shall be brought in the superior court.

perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the non-solicitation period to 10 years. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation state that Arizona courts scrutinize non-solicitation agreements for reasonableness, and a ten-year non-solicitation clause is potentially unreasonable and could violate Arizona's policy against restraints of trade if it is more than necessary to protect legitimate business interests. While the snippets provided don't directly address the reasonableness of non-solicitation agreements, the explanations suggest that the extended period could be deemed an unlawful restraint of trade under Arizona law, thus creating a contradiction by potentially violating legal principles related to fair competition and employee mobility.

perturbation.original_text: Notice shall be deemed effective and delivered three days after mailing if sent certified mail, return receipt requested, or when received if sent by electronic mail (e-mail), telecopy, prepaid courier, express mail or personal delivery to the intended recipient thereof at the address shown on the first page hereof with confirmation of delivery, or to such other address as either Party may specify in a written notice to the other Party pursuant hereto.
perturbation.changed_text: Notice shall be deemed effective and delivered ten days after mailing if sent certified mail, return receipt requested, or when received if sent by electronic mail (e-mail), telecopy, prepaid courier, express mail or personal delivery to the intended recipient thereof at the address shown on the first page hereof with confirmation of delivery, or to such other address as either Party may specify in a written notice to the other Party pursuant hereto.
perturbation.explanation: This change increases the time for notice to be effective after mailing to ten days. In Arizona, certain legal notices or filings may have specific deadlines tied to effective notice. A ten-day delay might cause a party to miss a statutory deadline, potentially violating procedural fairness or due process, depending on the context of the notice.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Arizona Rules of Civil Procedure pertaining to service of process and notice.
perturbation.law_citation: Rule 6(e) of the Arizona Rules of Civil Procedure
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 6(e) of the Arizona Rules of Civil Procedure addresses the computation of time and allows for additional time after service by mail. By changing the contractual notice period, the contract creates a different standard for notice than state law, which could cause an issue when calculating deadlines for legal process. The new language could create confusion as to whether the Rule 6(e) of the Arizona Rules of Civil Procedure is still applicable or the contractual deadline is now the governing one.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Message - Arizona Court Rules This document is not available on Westlaw.
perturbation.justification: The modified text increases the notice period from three to ten days. The Law Explanation says that this change creates a different standard for notice than state law, specifically mentioning Rule 6(e) of the Arizona Rules of Civil Procedure, which addresses computation of time and allows for additional time after service by mail. This suggests the ten-day period could conflict with legal deadlines, which establishes a contradiction because it creates a deadline that violates state law. While the provided snippets are unhelpful, the Law Explanation details a potential conflict between the contractual terms and existing legal procedure.
